questions.question: Two residents value a fireworks display. Alex's marginal benefit is MBA = 40 - Q and Brianna's is MBB = 30 - Q. Marginal cost is MC = 20. What is the efficient quantity of fireworks?
questions.explanation: For a public good, vertically sum marginal benefits: MSB = 70 - 2Q. Efficiency requires 70 - 2Q = 20, so Q = 25. B horizontally sums as if this were a private good. C uses only Alex's marginal benefit. D uses only Brianna's marginal benefit.

questions.question: Three roommates are deciding how many hours Q of apartment cleaning to buy. Their marginal benefits are 30 - Q, 20 - Q, and 10 - Q. The cleaner charges a constant MC = 24 per hour. What is the efficient number of hours, and why?

choices.A: Q = 12; because the sum of marginal benefits equals marginal cost.
choices.B: Q = 6; because the highest individual marginal benefit equals marginal cost.
choices.C: Q = 18; because total benefits should be horizontally summed.
choices.D: Q = 0; because no roommate individually values the first hour above $24.

questions.explanation: For shared cleaning, the same hour benefits all roommates, so sum marginal benefits: MSB = 60 - 3Q. Set 60 - 3Q = 24, so Q = 12. B uses only one person's benefit. C treats the public good like a private good. D is false because at Q = 0 the sum of benefits is 60.

questions.question: Two people have marginal benefits for a public radio station: MB1 = 50 - Q and MB2 = a - Q. MC = 30. What happens to efficient Q when a rises from 30 to 50?

choices.A: Q rises from 25 to 35 because the vertical sum of marginal benefits shifts upward.
choices.B: Q falls from 25 to 15 because more people free ride.
choices.C: Q stays at 20 because only the highest marginal benefit matters.
choices.D: Q rises from 20 to 40 because marginal costs shift downward.

questions.explanation: MSB = 50 - Q + a - Q = 50 + a - 2Q. Set equal to 30. If a = 30, Q = 25; if a = 50, Q = 35. The mechanism is an upward shift in aggregate marginal benefit. B confuses valuation with strategic contribution. C ignores vertical summation. D incorrectly shifts cost.

questions.question: For a public park, the sum of marginal benefits is MSB = 120 - 2Q and marginal cost is MC = c. What happens to efficient Q when c rises from 40 to 60?

choices.A: It rises from 40 to 60 because higher cost implies more valuable provision.
choices.B: It falls from 40 to 30 because the MC line shifts upward.
choices.C: It remains 60 because public goods are non-rival.
choices.D: It falls from 80 to 60 because individual benefits are summed horizontally.

questions.explanation: Efficiency sets 120 - 2Q = c. When c = 40, Q = 40; when c = 60, Q = 30. The mechanism is a higher marginal cost reducing the efficient provision level. A reverses the effect. C confuses non-rivalry with cost irrelevance. D uses incorrect quantities and summation logic.

questions.question: In a public good diagram, two individual marginal benefit curves slope downward. How is the social marginal benefit curve constructed?

choices.A: By vertically summing individual marginal benefits at each quantity.
choices.B: By horizontally summing quantities demanded at each price.
choices.C: By taking only the highest marginal benefit at each quantity.
choices.D: By averaging the two marginal benefit curves.

questions.explanation: Because all consumers can consume the same unit of a public good, social marginal benefit is the sum of their marginal willingnesses to pay for that unit. B describes private good demand aggregation. C ignores other beneficiaries. D undercounts total benefit unless there is only one consumer.

questions.question: A graph for lighthouse provision shows the vertically summed marginal benefit curve crossing MC at Q = 6, while no individual marginal benefit curve crosses MC above Q = 0. What does this show?

choices.A: Efficient provision is zero because no individual alone values the lighthouse enough.
choices.B: Efficient provision is positive because combined marginal benefits can exceed marginal cost even when individual benefits do not.
choices.C: The lighthouse is a private good because marginal benefits are downward sloping.
choices.D: The market will automatically provide Q = 6 through voluntary pricing.

questions.explanation: Public good efficiency depends on the sum of individual marginal benefits. A ignores aggregation. C confuses demand shape with rivalry and excludability. D ignores the free-rider problem.

questions.question: Why do public goods create a free-rider problem?

choices.A: Because one person's consumption reduces the amount available to others.
choices.B: Because non-excludability makes it hard to prevent non-payers from consuming the good.
choices.C: Because firms always have zero marginal cost for public goods.
choices.D: Because public goods have no marginal benefit.

questions.explanation: Non-excludability means people can benefit without paying, weakening voluntary contribution incentives. A describes rivalry, not public goods. C is not generally true. D is false; public goods can have high marginal benefits.

questions.question: A town is considering tornado sirens. Once installed, everyone nearby hears the warning, and one person's hearing it does not prevent others from hearing it. What model applies?

choices.A: Public good, because the siren warning is non-excludable and non-rival.
choices.B: Common resource, because everyone can use it and it gets depleted.
choices.C: Private good, because each resident has a separate willingness to pay.
choices.D: Negative externality, because sirens are loud.

questions.explanation: The warning is non-excludable and non-rival, so public good analysis applies. B is wrong because the warning is not depleted by use. C confuses individual valuation with private-good status. D focuses on a possible side effect, not the primary provision problem.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes non-rivalry from non-excludability?

choices.A: Non-rivalry means one person's consumption does not reduce availability to others; non-excludability means it is hard to prevent non-payers from consuming.
choices.B: Non-rivalry means people cannot be charged; non-excludability means marginal cost is positive.
choices.C: Non-rivalry means the good is supplied by government; non-excludability means it is supplied by markets.
choices.D: Non-rivalry means no one values the good; non-excludability means everyone values it equally.

questions.explanation: Rivalry concerns whether consumption depletes the good; excludability concerns whether access can be restricted. B swaps and distorts the concepts. C incorrectly defines them by provider. D invents valuation assumptions.

questions.question: Two households value street lighting with MB1 = 60 - Q and MB2 = 40 - Q. MC = 40. If voluntary private provision supplies Q = 20 but the efficient quantity is Q = 30, what is the deadweight loss from underprovision?
questions.explanation: MSB = 100 - 2Q. At Q = 20, MSB = 60; at Q = 30, MSB = 40 = MC. DWL is the triangle between MSB and MC from 20 to 30: 0.5 × 10 × 20 = 100. B doubles the triangle. C uses the wrong height. D uses total cost over the missing units.
